docs.title: DBWarden
docs.text:
DBWarden is a SQL-first migration system for Python + SQLAlchemy projects.

It is built for teams that want migration changes to stay explicit, reviewable, and safe from local development to production.

docs.title: What DBWarden Is
docs.text:
• A migration workflow centered on SQL files you can review
  • A CLI for generating, applying, rolling back, and auditing migrations
  • A multi-database migration tool with lock and checksum safety built in

docs.title: What DBWarden Is Not
docs.text:
• An ORM replacement
  • A hidden auto-migration engine that mutates schema silently
  • A deployment platform

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Explicit --upgrade and --rollback SQL sections in every migration
  • Multi-database support from one config source
  • Dev mode (--dev) with optional SQLite translation workflow
  • Locking and checksum integrity checks
  • Status/history visibility for release and incident workflows

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • SQLAlchemy models for model-driven migration generation
  • A supported database backend (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, SQLite, ClickHouse)

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install dbwarden
dbwarden init

docs.title: 60-Second Workflow
docs.text:
# 1) Initialize project
dbwarden init

# 2) Generate SQL migration from models
dbwarden make-migrations "create users table" --database primary

# 3) Apply migrations
dbwarden migrate --database primary

# 4) Verify state
dbwarden status --database primary

docs.title: Minimal Config Example
docs.text:
from dbwarden import database_config


database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main",
    model_paths=["app/models"],
)

docs.title: Example Upgrade (Dev Loop)
docs.text:
# Generate and apply against dev database
dbwarden --dev make-migrations "add indexes" --database primary
dbwarden --dev migrate --database primary

# Validate status/history
dbwarden --dev status --database primary
dbwarden --dev history --database primary

docs.title: Why Teams Choose This Model
docs.text:
• SQL is the source of truth
  • Rollback is mandatory, not optional
  • One migration flow works for one DB or many DBs
  • Operational safety is first-class (locks, checksums, auditable history)

docs.title: Recap
docs.text:
With one database_config(...) definition and one migration command loop, you get:

  • typed config validation
  • deterministic migration plans
  • explicit SQL artifacts in version control
  • safe execution with recovery-oriented tooling

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: This page explains DBWarden internals for contributors and advanced debugging.

docs.title: Layered architecture
docs.text:
CLI (Typer)
  -> Commands layer
    -> Engine layer (planning/parsing/version/checksum/model discovery)
      -> Repository layer (migration + lock records)
        -> Database layer (SQLAlchemy connection + SQL execution)

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
• CLI: parse args, global flags (--dev, --strict-translation)
  • Commands: orchestrate workflows (migrate, rollback, make-migrations)
  • Engine: parse files, resolve ordering, checksums, model discovery
  • Repositories: read/write migration and lock metadata
  • Database: execute SQL with backend-aware connections

docs.title: Configuration resolution pipeline
docs.text:
When runtime config is requested:

  1. discover one config source (dbwarden.py or single callsite)
  2. fallback to DBWARDEN_CONFIG_MODULE when configured
  3. import source and execute database_config(...) calls
  4. validate uniqueness/default/model-path rules
  5. resolve selected database and apply --dev swap when enabled

Ambiguous sources fail fast.

docs.title: Migration execution lifecycle
docs.text:
For migrate:

  1. ensure migrations metadata table exists
  2. ensure lock table exists
  3. acquire lock
  4. build pending execution plan
  5. execute SQL statements
  6. record migration metadata/checksums
  7. release lock

docs.title: Rollback lifecycle
docs.text: Rollback uses the same lock discipline, selecting rollback SQL from applied files in reverse order.

docs.title: Model-to-SQL generation lifecycle
docs.text:
make-migrations pipeline:

  1. discover model paths
  2. import model modules
  3. extract table/column metadata
  4. compare against known schema state (database + migration history)
  5. generate upgrade and rollback SQL
  6. write migration file

docs.title: Repeatable migration model
docs.text:
Supported classes:

  • versioned (NNNN_): run once in ordered sequence
  • runs always (RA__): run each migrate execution
  • runs on change (ROC__): run only when checksum changes

docs.title: Integrity model
docs.text:
Checksums are recorded for migration content and used for:

  • repeatable migration change detection
  • migration consistency checks
  • audit/debug confidence

docs.title: Concurrency model
docs.text:
Migration-mutating commands are serialized by lock state stored in database tables.

Recovery commands:

  • dbwarden lock-status
  • dbwarden unlock

docs.title: Dev translation path
docs.text:
With --dev and SQLite target:

  1. extract model types/defaults
  2. translate backend-specific constructs
  3. fallback behavior in non-strict mode
  4. fail-fast behavior with --strict-translation

Translation happens during SQL generation, not by mutating existing migration files.

docs.title: Error propagation strategy
docs.text:
• config/load validation errors fail early
  • execution errors abort current run with context
  • lock release is guarded in cleanup paths

docs.title: Quick Reference (Legacy)
docs.text:
DBWarden uses Python configuration through database_config(...) calls.

For complete documentation, see the Configuration Guide.

docs.title: Single database example
docs.text:
from dbwarden import database_config


database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main",
    migrations_dir="migrations/primary",
)

docs.title: Multi-database example
docs.text:
from dbwarden import database_config


database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main",
    model_paths=["app/models/api"],
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="clickhouse",
    database_url="clickhouse://user:password@localhost:8123/analytics",
    model_paths=["app/models/metrics"],
)

docs.title: Dev database example
docs.text:
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./development.db",
)

Use with:

dbwarden --dev migrate --database primary

docs.title: Source resolution
docs.text:
DBWarden resolves config source in this order:

  1. discovered dbwarden.py
  2. full scan for files containing database_config(...)
  3. DBWARDEN_CONFIG_MODULE environment variable

If more than one discovered source exists, DBWarden fails fast with an ambiguity error.

docs.title: Validation rules
docs.text:
• exactly one default=True
  • duplicate database names are rejected
  • duplicate URL/target collisions are rejected
  • if more than one database is configured, model_paths is required on each
  • overlapping model_paths require explicit overlap_models=True

docs.title: Secure display mode
docs.text:
Set secure_values=True when you want display commands to show variable expressions instead of resolved values for non-literal arguments.

DATABASE_URL = "postgresql://..."

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=DATABASE_URL,
    secure_values=True,
)

Reference: Configuration API

docs.title: Supported Databases
docs.text: DBWarden supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, SQLite, and ClickHouse.

docs.title: Backend Matrix
docs.text: Backend database_type Typical URL PostgreSQL postgresql postgresql://user:pass@host:5432/db MySQL mysql mysql://user:pass@host:3306/db MariaDB mariadb mariadb://user:pass@host:3306/db SQLite sqlite sqlite:///./app.db ClickHouse clickhouse clickhouse://user:pass@host:8123/db

docs.title: Config Examples
docs.text:
PostgreSQL:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main",
)

MySQL:

database_config(
    database_name="legacy",
    database_type="mysql",
    database_url="mysql://user:password@localhost:3306/legacy",
)

SQLite:

database_config(
    database_name="dev",
    database_type="sqlite",
    database_url="sqlite:///./development.db",
)

ClickHouse:

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="clickhouse",
    database_url="clickhouse://user:password@localhost:8123/analytics",
)

docs.title: Internal Connection Handling
docs.text:
DBWarden uses SQLAlchemy engines, with backend-specific URL normalization where needed.

Conceptual flow:

def get_engine(config):
    url = config.sqlalchemy_url
    if config.database_type == "clickhouse":
        url = normalize_clickhouse_dialect(url)
    return create_engine(url)

For PostgreSQL schema support, DBWarden sets search_path on connection when postgres_schema is configured.

docs.title: Development Database Strategy
docs.text:
Recommended pattern:

  • Production-like primary DB (for example PostgreSQL)
  • SQLite for dev DB via dev_database_url
  • Run local commands with --dev
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./development.db",
)

dbwarden --dev make-migrations "sync models" -d primary
dbwarden --dev migrate -d primary

docs.title: Translation Note
docs.text:
When targeting SQLite in dev mode, DBWarden translates unsupported backend-specific types/defaults.

  • Unknown/unsupported types fallback to TEXT with warnings
  • --strict-translation turns those warnings into errors

Details: SQL Translation

docs.title: Backend-Specific Notes
docs.text:
PostgreSQL:

  • Strong support for JSONB, UUID, rich DDL
  • Prefer production runs on actual PostgreSQL instance

MySQL/MariaDB:

  • Similar behavior; MariaDB configured separately via database_type
  • Validate engine-specific syntax in manual migrations

SQLite:

  • Great for local tests and dev loops
  • Different type affinity and DDL limitations vs server databases

ClickHouse:

  • Optimized for analytics workloads
  • Use manual SQL for engine/partition/order specifics when needed

docs.title: Recommended Verification Workflow
docs.text:
# local loop on dev DB
dbwarden --dev migrate -d primary

# pre-release validation on production-like DB
dbwarden migrate -d primary
dbwarden status -d primary

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide covers installing DBWarden in your project and verifying it works correctly.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10 or higher
  • A project that uses SQLAlchemy for database models
  • pip or another Python package manager

docs.title: Install using pip
docs.text: pip install dbwarden

docs.title: With poetry
docs.text:
If your project uses Poetry:

poetry add dbwarden

docs.title: Development dependencies
docs.text:
To also install testing and linting tools:

pip install "dbwarden[dev]"

Or with poetry:

poetry add --group dev dbwarden

docs.title: Database drivers
docs.text:
DBWarden uses SQLAlchemy under the hood. Your project already has a database driver, but you can ensure specific drivers:

# PostgreSQL (most common)
pip install psycopg2-binary

# MySQL/MariaDB
pip install mysql-connector-python

# SQLite comes bundled with Python

docs.title: Verify installation
docs.text:
After installing, confirm DBWarden is available:

dbwarden version

You should see output:

0.5

docs.title: Initialize in your project
docs.text:
Create the DBWarden structure in your project directory:

dbwarden init

This creates:

  • a migrations/ directory structure
  • a dbwarden.py config scaffold (or discovers your existing config source)

docs.title: What happens during init
docs.text:
When you run init, DBWarden:

  1. Creates migrations/ if missing
  2. Creates dbwarden.py (or updates existing config source) with import scaffolding
  3. Does not overwrite existing database_config(...) definitions you have added

You can run init safely on an existing project - it is idempotent.

docs.title: Quick configuration
docs.text:
After init, configure your first database by editing dbwarden.py (or your existing config file that contains database_config(...) calls):

from dbwarden import database_config


database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/mydb",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./development.db",
)

The dev_database_* fields are optional but recommended - they enable fast local iterations with --dev.

docs.title: Verify configuration loads
docs.text:
dbwarden settings show --all

You should see your database entry printed with type and URL.

docs.title: Common installation issues
docs.text:
Command not found after pip install

  • Ensure your virtual environment is activated
  • Try uninstalling and reinstalling: pip uninstall dbwarden && pip install dbwarden

Import errors or missing module warnings

  • Upgrade pip and reinstall: pip install --upgrade pip dbwarden

Database driver errors

  • Install the appropriate driver for your target database (see Database drivers section above)

docs.title: Upgrading
docs.text:
To update to a newer version:

pip install --upgrade dbwarden

Or with poetry:

poetry update dbwarden

Check the release notes when upgrading major versions - there may be configuration or workflow changes.

docs.title: Migration File Format
docs.text:
Migration files are the execution contract in DBWarden.

Everything that changes your database should be represented in explicit SQL files that can be reviewed, tested, and rolled back.

docs.title: File naming and location
docs.text:
Versioned migrations are stored under each database migrations directory (default: migrations/<database_name>).

Canonical filename pattern:

{database_name}__{version}_{description}.sql

Examples:

primary__0001_initial_schema.sql
primary__0002_add_users_table.sql
analytics__0001_create_events.sql

docs.title: Required sections
docs.text:
Each migration file must define both:

-- upgrade

-- rollback
  • -- upgrade: statements applied during migrate
  • -- rollback: statements applied during rollback

If rollback is weak or incomplete, production recovery is weak or incomplete.

docs.title: Migration classes
docs.text:
DBWarden supports three execution classes:

Prefix Class Behavior NNNN_ Versioned Runs once in ordered version sequence RA__ Runs always Runs on every migrate execution ROC__ Runs on change Runs when checksum changed

docs.title: When to use each
docs.text:
• NNNN_: schema evolution (tables, columns, indexes, constraints)
  • RA__: objects that should always be refreshed (views, grants)
  • ROC__: routines/policies that should apply only when content changes

docs.title: Execution model
docs.text:
At runtime, DBWarden builds a plan from file discovery + migration metadata:

  1. read versioned files and filter already-applied versions
  2. include RA__ files
  3. include changed ROC__ files
  4. execute with lock protection
  5. record metadata and checksums

Conceptual plan:

def build_plan(directory, applied_versions):
    versioned = parse_versioned_files(directory)
    repeatable = parse_repeatable_files(directory)
    pending_versioned = [m for m in versioned if m.version not in applied_versions]
    pending_ra = repeatable.runs_always
    pending_roc = changed_only(repeatable.runs_on_change)
    return pending_versioned + pending_ra + pending_roc

docs.title: Versioned migration
docs.text:
-- upgrade

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    created_at DATETIME
);

-- rollback

DROP TABLE users;

docs.title: Runs-always migration (RA__)
docs.text:
Filename example: primary__RA__refresh_active_users_view.sql

-- upgrade

CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW active_users AS
SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE is_active = TRUE;

-- rollback

DROP VIEW IF EXISTS active_users;

docs.title: Runs-on-change migration (ROC__)
docs.text:
Filename example: primary__ROC__update_timestamp_trigger.sql

-- upgrade

CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION update_updated_at()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  NEW.updated_at = NOW();
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- rollback

DROP FUNCTION IF EXISTS update_updated_at();

docs.title: Metadata headers
docs.text:
Headers are parsed before SQL execution and can influence ordering/behavior.

Dependency header:

-- depends_on: ["0004", "0005"]

Seed marker:

-- seed

docs.title: Authoring guidelines
docs.text:
• One logical change per migration file
  • Keep DDL explicit; avoid hidden application-side schema effects
  • Keep rollback idempotent when possible (IF EXISTS, safe predicates)
  • For data migrations, use bounded, reversible operations
  • Prefer small migrations over large monolithic SQL scripts

docs.title: Review checklist
docs.text:
Before merge:

  • upgrade section matches intended schema change
  • rollback section restores prior valid state
  • indexes/constraints/defaults are explicit
  • no environment-specific literals accidentally committed

Before release:

dbwarden status --database primary
dbwarden migrate --database primary
dbwarden rollback --database primary --count 1
dbwarden migrate --database primary

docs.title: SQLAlchemy Models
docs.text:
DBWarden reads SQLAlchemy model metadata to generate migration SQL.

Use model_paths in your database_config(...) entries to control discovery.

Example:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5432/main",
    model_paths=["app/models"],
)

Related docs:

  • Configuration
  • Your First Migration

docs.title: SQL Translation
docs.text:
DBWarden includes a SQL translation layer to support development workflows where your primary database differs from your development database.

The most common case is:

  • Primary database: PostgreSQL/MySQL/MariaDB/ClickHouse
  • Development database: SQLite (--dev mode)

This keeps local development fast while still allowing production-targeted schemas.

docs.title: Why SQL Translation Exists
docs.text:
SQLite does not support all backend-specific SQL types and default expressions used by other databases.

Without translation, generated migrations can fail in local development when they contain backend-specific types like UUID, JSONB, or default expressions like now().

DBWarden translation solves this by adapting generated SQL for SQLite compatibility.

docs.title: When translation is active
docs.text:
Translation is applied when all are true:

  • command runs with --dev
  • selected database resolves to a SQLite dev_database_url
  • command path generates SQL from models (make-migrations)

It is not a runtime SQL proxy for arbitrary manual SQL.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
When you run commands in development mode and target a SQLite dev database:

dbwarden --dev make-migrations "sync models" -d primary

DBWarden uses this flow:

  1. Loads the selected database config and resolves dev_database_url.
  2. Detects that the active target backend is SQLite.
  3. Extracts model metadata from SQLAlchemy models.
  4. Translates backend-specific types/defaults to SQLite-compatible SQL.
  5. Generates migration SQL with translated definitions.

Translation is applied during migration generation, not as a post-processing regex pass.

docs.title: Type conversion behavior
docs.text:
Common conversions:

Source type SQLite output UUID TEXT JSON / JSONB TEXT TIMESTAMPTZ DATETIME SERIAL / BIGSERIAL INTEGER ClickHouse nullable numeric forms INTEGER/REAL depending on source

If a type cannot be translated safely:

  • non-strict mode: fallback to TEXT + warning
  • strict mode: fail migration generation

docs.title: Type Translation Behavior
docs.text:
DBWarden maps many backend-specific types to SQLite-compatible types.

Examples:

  • UUID -> TEXT
  • JSON / JSONB -> TEXT
  • TIMESTAMPTZ -> DATETIME
  • SERIAL / BIGSERIAL -> INTEGER
  • Nullable(UInt64) (ClickHouse) -> INTEGER

For unsupported or unknown types that cannot be converted safely:

  • DBWarden falls back to TEXT
  • Logs a warning indicating the fallback

docs.title: Default expression handling
docs.text:
Backend expressions such as now() or gen_random_uuid() may not have direct SQLite equivalents.

In non-strict mode, unsupported defaults are dropped with warning.

In strict mode, unsupported defaults fail generation.

docs.title: Default Expression Translation
docs.text:
DBWarden also handles unsupported default expressions.

Examples of expressions that may be removed for SQLite compatibility:

  • now()
  • gen_random_uuid()
  • sequence-based defaults (like nextval(...))

In non-strict mode, unsupported defaults are removed and a warning is logged.

docs.title: Strict Translation Mode
docs.text:
If you want hard failures instead of fallback behavior:

dbwarden --dev --strict-translation make-migrations "sync models" -d primary

In strict mode:

  • Unknown/unsupported type conversions raise errors
  • Unsupported default expression conversions raise errors

Use this when you want to catch every lossy conversion early.

docs.title: Recommended team workflow
docs.text:
1. iterate quickly with --dev (SQLite)
  2. keep strict checks in CI (--strict-translation)
  3. validate release candidate migrations against production-like database

This balances speed and correctness.

docs.title: Recommended Setup
docs.text:
For fast local testing and predictable developer workflows, use SQLite as your dev database:

from dbwarden import database_config


database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/main",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./development.db",
)

Then run development commands with:

dbwarden --dev migrate -d primary
dbwarden --dev make-migrations "add indexes" -d primary

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
--dev mode is enabled, but database '<name>' has no dev_database_url configured:

  • add dev_database_url for that database entry

Unexpected type fallback to TEXT:

  • inspect model type for backend-specific declaration
  • re-run with --strict-translation to fail fast and fix explicitly

Generated SQL differs from production expectations:

  • expected in SQLite compatibility mode; validate final release migrations on production-like backend

docs.title: Notes and Limitations
docs.text:
• Translation focuses on compatibility for local development.
  • Some backend features cannot be represented exactly in SQLite.
  • For production accuracy, always test migrations against your production-like database too.

docs.title: Checksum Integrity
docs.text:
DBWarden stores checksums for executed migrations.

Checksums are used to:

  • detect changed migration content
  • support repeatable migration behavior (RA__, ROC__)
  • avoid accidental re-application of equivalent SQL

Best practice: do not edit already-applied versioned migration files.

docs.title: CI/CD Patterns
docs.text: Use a consistent migration stage in pipelines.

docs.title: Minimal pattern
docs.text:
- run: dbwarden status --database primary
- run: dbwarden migrate --database primary
- run: dbwarden status --database primary

docs.title: Recommendations
docs.text:
• run migrations from one job only
  • fail pipeline on non-zero migration exit
  • store migration logs/artifacts for audit

docs.title: Migration Locking
docs.text: DBWarden uses migration locks to prevent concurrent schema mutation.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
dbwarden lock-status --database primary
dbwarden unlock --database primary

docs.title: Stale lock recovery
docs.text:
1. Confirm no migration process is running.
  2. Check lock status.
  3. Release lock with unlock.
  4. Retry migrate.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text:
• Previous: SQL Translation
  • Next: Checksum Integrity

docs.title: Safe Deployment
docs.text: Use a predictable release sequence for schema changes.

docs.title: Standard flow
docs.text:
dbwarden status --database primary
dbwarden migrate --database primary --with-backup
dbwarden history --database primary

For multi-database deployments:

dbwarden migrate --all --with-backup

docs.title: Recovery checklist
docs.text:
1. Check lock status
  2. Inspect history and pending state
  3. Decide rollback vs forward-fix
  4. Execute one controlled change

docs.text:
• Previous: CI/CD Patterns
  • Next: CLI Reference

docs.title: Squashing Migrations
docs.text: Squashing consolidates multiple migrations into a smaller set after a stable release boundary.

docs.title: Command
docs.text:
dbwarden squash --database primary

Use squashing only when:

  • no pending migrations remain
  • your team agrees on a stable checkpoint
  • rollback strategy remains clear

docs.title: When to squash
docs.text: After multiple versioned migrations that have been stable through several releases, you may want to consolidate them to reduce file count and simplify rollback.

docs.title: Workflow recommendation
docs.text:
1. Confirm all migrations are applied and stable
  2. Run squash and review new consolidated file
  3. Test that rollback still works correctly
  4. Keep the new file, retire the old ones in version control

docs.title: init
docs.text: Initialize DBWarden project scaffolding.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:

docs.title: make-migrations
docs.text: Generate SQL migration file(s) from SQLAlchemy models.

dbwarden migrate --database primary --baseline --to-version 0005

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
DBWarden uses Python-based configuration with database_config() to define your databases.

One configuration source for migrations, CLI tools, and runtime → no split configs.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
The simplest configuration possible:

# dbwarden.py
from dbwarden import database_config

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="sqlite",
    database_url="sqlite:///./app.db",
)

That's it! 4 parameters to get started.

Run your first migration:

dbwarden init
dbwarden make-migrations "initial schema"
dbwarden migrate

docs.title: 🚀 Simple Configuration
docs.text:
Define once, use everywhere:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
    model_paths=["app.models"],
)

docs.title: 🔧 Dev Mode
docs.text:
Use SQLite locally, PostgreSQL in production:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev.db",
)

Run commands with --dev:

dbwarden --dev migrate
dbwarden --dev status

docs.title: 🗄️ Multi-Database
docs.text:
Configure as many databases as you need:

# Primary database
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/main",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],
)

# Analytics database
database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="clickhouse",
    database_url="http://localhost:8123/analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],
)

docs.title: 🔒 Security First
docs.text:
Keep credentials out of code:

import os

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=os.getenv("DATABASE_URL"),
    secure_values=True,  # Hide credentials in output
)

docs.title: ✅ Validation
docs.text:
DBWarden validates your configuration:

  • ✅ Exactly one default=True
  • ✅ Unique database names
  • ✅ No duplicate URLs
  • ✅ Required model_paths for multi-database
  • ✅ Consistent dev mode configuration

docs.title: Configuration Loading
docs.text:
DBWarden discovers your configuration automatically:

  1. Looks for dbwarden.py in current directory or parents
  2. Scans for database_config() calls in your codebase
  3. Checks DBWARDEN_CONFIG_MODULE environment variable

Place dbwarden.py in your project root for best results.

docs.text:
from dbwarden import database_config

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
)

docs.text:
from dbwarden import database_config

database_config(

    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev.db",
    model_paths=["app.models"],

docs.title: Multiple Databases
docs.text:
from dbwarden import database_config

# Primary
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/main",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],
)

# Analytics
database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],

docs.title: Production with Environment Variables
docs.text:
import os
from dbwarden import database_config

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=os.getenv("DATABASE_URL"),
    model_paths=["app.models"],
    secure_values=True,
)

docs.title: Why Python Configuration?
docs.text:
vs TOML/YAML/INI: - ✅ Type checking with your IDE - ✅ Dynamic configuration (loops, conditionals) - ✅ Environment variable integration - ✅ No schema mismatches - ✅ Can compute values

vs Environment Variables Only: - ✅ Version controlled - ✅ Self-documenting - ✅ Validation at load time - ✅ Multiple databases easy - ✅ Can reference code structures

docs.title: Configuration Concepts
docs.text: Understand how DBWarden configuration works under the hood.

docs.title: What is Configuration?
docs.text: Configuration tells DBWarden: - Where your databases are (connection URLs) - What kind of databases they are (PostgreSQL, SQLite, etc.) - Where your SQLAlchemy models live (for migration generation) - Where to store migrations (directories)

docs.title: Type Safety
docs.text:
Your IDE can help you:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",  # ← IDE suggests parameter names
    default=True,             # ← IDE knows this is boolean
    database_type="sqlite",   # ← IDE can validate enum values
    database_url="...",
)

docs.title: Dynamic Configuration
docs.text:
You can use Python logic:

import os

# Different config per environment
environment = os.getenv("ENV", "dev")

if environment == "production":
    database_url = "postgresql://prod-host/myapp"
else:
    database_url = "sqlite:///./dev.db"

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql" if environment == "production" else "sqlite",
    database_url=database_url,
)

docs.title: Multiple Databases
docs.text:
Easy to configure multiple databases:

DATABASES = {
    "primary": "postgresql://localhost/main",
    "analytics": "postgresql://localhost/analytics",
    "logging": "postgresql://localhost/logs",
}

for name, url in DATABASES.items():
    database_config(
        database_name=name,
        default=(name == "primary"),
        database_type="postgresql",
        database_url=url,
        model_paths=[f"app.models.{name}"],
    )

docs.title: Discovery Order
docs.text:
DBWarden searches for configuration in this order:

1. dbwarden.py in current directory
     ↓ not found
2. dbwarden.py in parent directories
     ↓ not found
3. Full scan for files with database_config()
     ↓ not found
4. DBWARDEN_CONFIG_MODULE environment variable
     ↓ not found
Error: No configuration found

docs.title: When Configuration Loads
docs.text:
Configuration loads when you run any DBWarden command:

dbwarden migrate    # ← Config loads here
dbwarden status     # ← Config loads here
dbwarden history    # ← Config loads here

Load process: 1. Python imports your config module 2. database_config() calls execute 3. Databases register in internal registry 4. Validation runs 5. Command executes with loaded config

docs.title: Validation Rules
docs.text:
DBWarden validates configuration at load time:

Rule Why It Matters Exactly one default=True CLI needs to know which DB to use when --database is omitted Unique database_name Commands target databases by name Unique database_url Prevents accidental duplicate configurations Unique physical targets Prevents two configs pointing to same DB with different credentials Required model_paths in multi-DB Keeps model discovery boundaries clear No overlapping model_paths Prevents ambiguous model ownership (unless overlap_models=True)

docs.title: Validation Timing
docs.text:
# dbwarden.py
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
)

database_config(
    database_name="primary",  # ← Duplicate!
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/other",
)

When you run dbwarden migrate:

Error: Duplicate database_name 'primary'

Validation happens before any commands execute.

docs.title: Why default=True Exists
docs.text:
Consider these commands:

# Explicit database
dbwarden migrate --database primary

# Implicit database (uses default)
dbwarden migrate

Without default=True, DBWarden wouldn't know which database to use for the second command.

docs.title: Only One Default
docs.text:
# ✅ Good
database_config(database_name="primary", default=True, ...)
database_config(database_name="analytics", default=False, ...)  # or omit default

# ❌ Bad - two defaults
database_config(database_name="primary", default=True, ...)
database_config(database_name="analytics", default=True, ...)  # Error!

docs.title: Default Affects CLI Behavior
docs.text:
# These are equivalent when primary is default:
dbwarden migrate
dbwarden migrate --database primary

# These are NOT equivalent:
dbwarden migrate
dbwarden migrate --database analytics  # Targets analytics, not primary

docs.title: What Are model_paths?
docs.text:
model_paths tells DBWarden where your SQLAlchemy models live:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
    model_paths=["app.models"],  # ← Look here for models
)

docs.title: How Discovery Works
docs.text:
1. Import each module in model_paths
     ↓
2. Find all classes inheriting from DeclarativeBase
     ↓
3. Extract table metadata (__tablename__, columns, etc.)
     ↓
4. Build internal representation for migration generation

docs.title: When Is It Required?
docs.text:
Single database: Optional (DBWarden scans entire codebase)

# This works
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="sqlite",
    database_url="sqlite:///./app.db",
    # No model_paths needed
)

Multiple databases: Required for each database

# This is required
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/main",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],  # ← Required
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],  # ← Required
)

Why? To prevent ambiguity about which models belong to which database.

docs.title: What Is Dev Mode?
docs.text:
Dev mode lets you use a different database for local development:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",              # Production
    database_url="postgresql://prod/myapp",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",              # Development
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev.db",
)

Run commands with --dev:

dbwarden --dev migrate  # Uses SQLite
dbwarden migrate        # Uses PostgreSQL

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Command: dbwarden --dev migrate
    ↓
Check for --dev flag
    ↓
Swap database_type → dev_database_type
Swap database_url → dev_database_url
    ↓
Connect to dev database
    ↓
Execute command

docs.title: Why Use It?
docs.text:
Speed: - SQLite is faster than PostgreSQL for local iteration - No network latency - No server setup

Safety: - Can't accidentally affect production - Each developer has their own isolated database - Easy to reset (just delete the file)

Simplicity: - No Docker containers needed - No database server installation - Works on all platforms

docs.title: Why Multiple Databases?
docs.text: Common scenarios: - Separation of concerns - Transactions vs analytics - Performance - Offload reporting to separate database - Compliance - Audit logs in separate database - Legacy systems - New and old databases coexist

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Each database_config() call registers an independent database:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/main",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],
)

They're completely independent: - Separate migration histories - Separate migration directories - Separate model sets - Can use different database types

docs.title: Model Path Boundaries
docs.text:
app/
  models/
    primary/
      user.py         # ← Goes to primary database
      order.py
    analytics/
      event.py        # ← Goes to analytics database
      metric.py

Configuration:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],  # ← Only primary models
    ...
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],  # ← Only analytics models
    ...
)

docs.title: What Is secure_values?
docs.text:
Prevents credentials from appearing in terminal output:

import os

DATABASE_URL = os.getenv("DATABASE_URL")

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=DATABASE_URL,
    secure_values=True,  # ← Hide credentials
)

docs.title: Without secure_values:
docs.text:
$ dbwarden database
Database: primary
  URL: postgresql://user:SECRET_PASSWORD@prod-host/myapp

docs.title: With secure_values:
docs.text:
$ dbwarden database
Database: primary
  URL: DATABASE_URL (expression)

Shows the variable name instead of resolved value.

docs.title: Configuration Time
docs.text: When config loads: - database_config() calls execute - Validation runs - Internal registry populates - No database connections made

docs.title: Runtime
docs.text:
When commands run: - DBWarden reads from registry - Connects to database - Executes command logic

Key point: Configuration errors are caught early, before any database operations.

docs.text:

docs.title: Connection URLs
docs.text: Complete reference for database connection URL formats.

docs.title: URL Format
docs.text:
All database URLs follow this general structure:

[dialect[+driver]]://[username[:password]@][host][:port][/database][?option=value&...]

docs.title: Basic Format
docs.text: postgresql://[user[:password]@][host][:port]/database[?options]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Local default:

database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp"

With credentials:

database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/myapp"

Remote host:

database_url="postgresql://user:password@db.example.com:5432/myapp"

With SSL:

database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost/myapp?sslmode=require"

With connection pool:

database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost/myapp?pool_size=20&max_overflow=10"

docs.title: SSL Modes
docs.text:
Mode Description disable No SSL allow Try SSL, fall back to non-SSL prefer Try SSL first (default) require Require SSL, fail if unavailable verify-ca Require SSL + verify CA verify-full Require SSL + verify CA + hostname

Example:

database_url="postgresql://user:pass@host/db?sslmode=verify-full&sslrootcert=/path/to/ca.pem"

docs.title: Common Options
docs.text: Option Description Example sslmode SSL connection mode sslmode=require sslcert Client certificate sslcert=/path/to/cert.pem sslkey Client key sslkey=/path/to/key.pem sslrootcert CA certificate sslrootcert=/path/to/ca.pem connect_timeout Connection timeout (seconds) connect_timeout=10 application_name App name in pg_stat_activity application_name=myapp

docs.title: Cloud Providers
docs.text:
AWS RDS:

database_url="postgresql://user:pass@mydb.abc123.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com:5432/myapp?sslmode=require"

Google Cloud SQL:

database_url="postgresql://user:pass@/myapp?host=/cloudsql/project:region:instance"

Azure Database:

database_url="postgresql://user@server:pass@server.postgres.database.azure.com:5432/myapp?sslmode=require"

Heroku:

import os
database_url=os.getenv("DATABASE_URL")  # Provided by Heroku

docs.title: Basic Format
docs.text: sqlite:///[path]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Relative path:

database_url="sqlite:///./app.db"
database_url="sqlite:///./data/app.db"

Absolute path:

database_url="sqlite:////absolute/path/to/app.db"

In-memory (testing only):

database_url="sqlite:///:memory:"

In-Memory Databases

In-memory databases are lost when the connection closes. Only use for testing.

docs.title: Common Options
docs.text:
Option Description Example timeout Lock timeout (seconds) ?timeout=20 check_same_thread Thread safety check ?check_same_thread=false

Example:

database_url="sqlite:///./app.db?timeout=20"

docs.title: Basic Format
docs.text: mysql://[user[:password]@][host][:port]/database[?options]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Local:

database_url="mysql://root:password@localhost:3306/myapp"

With charset:

database_url="mysql://user:pass@localhost/myapp?charset=utf8mb4"

With SSL:

database_url="mysql://user:pass@localhost/myapp?ssl_ca=/path/to/ca.pem"

docs.title: Common Options
docs.text: Option Description Example charset Character set charset=utf8mb4 ssl_ca CA certificate ssl_ca=/path/to/ca.pem ssl_cert Client certificate ssl_cert=/path/to/cert.pem ssl_key Client key ssl_key=/path/to/key.pem

docs.title: MariaDB
docs.text:
MariaDB uses the same URL format as MySQL:

database_url="mysql://user:pass@localhost:3306/myapp"

Configure with database_type="mariadb":

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    database_type="mariadb",
    database_url="mysql://localhost/myapp",
)

docs.title: Basic Format
docs.text: http://[user[:password]@]host[:port]/database[?options]

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Local:

database_url="http://default:@localhost:8123/myapp"

With authentication:

database_url="http://user:password@localhost:8123/myapp"

With HTTPS:

database_url="https://user:password@clickhouse.example.com:8443/myapp"

docs.title: Common Options
docs.text:
Option Description Example compression Enable compression compression=1 connect_timeout Connection timeout connect_timeout=10 send_timeout Send timeout send_timeout=300 receive_timeout Receive timeout receive_timeout=300

Example:

database_url="http://user:pass@localhost:8123/myapp?compression=1&connect_timeout=10"

docs.title: Basic Pattern
docs.text:
import os

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=os.getenv("DATABASE_URL"),
)

docs.title: With Fallback
docs.text:
import os

database_url = os.getenv("DATABASE_URL", "sqlite:///./dev.db")

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql" if "postgresql" in database_url else "sqlite",
    database_url=database_url,
)

docs.title: Required Environment Variables
docs.text:
import os

DATABASE_URL = os.getenv("DATABASE_URL")
if not DATABASE_URL:
    raise ValueError("DATABASE_URL environment variable is required")

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=DATABASE_URL,

docs.title: Multiple Databases
docs.text:
import os

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=os.getenv("PRIMARY_DATABASE_URL"),
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=os.getenv("ANALYTICS_DATABASE_URL"),
)

docs.title: Special Characters
docs.text:
If your password contains special characters, URL-encode them:

Character Encoded @ %40 : %3A / %2F ? %3F # %23 & %26 % %25

Example:

Password: p@ss:word

database_url="postgresql://user:p%40ss%3Aword@localhost/myapp"

docs.title: Python URL Encoding
docs.text:
from urllib.parse import quote_plus

username = "user"
password = "p@ss:word"
host = "localhost"
database = "myapp"

database_url = f"postgresql://{username}:{quote_plus(password)}@{host}/{database}"
# Result: postgresql://user:p%40ss%3Aword@localhost/myapp

docs.title: PostgreSQL Pool Options
docs.text:
database_url="postgresql://user:pass@localhost/myapp?pool_size=20&max_overflow=10&pool_timeout=30"
Option Description Default pool_size Max connections in pool 5 max_overflow Extra connections if pool full 10 pool_timeout Wait time for connection (seconds) 30 pool_recycle Recycle connections after (seconds) -1 (never)

docs.title: Connection Lifetime
docs.text:
Recycle connections after 1 hour:

database_url="postgresql://user:pass@localhost/myapp?pool_recycle=3600"

docs.title: Verify URL Format
docs.text:
from sqlalchemy import create_engine

try:
    engine = create_engine("postgresql://user:pass@localhost/myapp")
    with engine.connect() as conn:
        result = conn.execute("SELECT 1")
        print("Connection successful!")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Connection failed: {e}")

docs.title: Test with DBWarden
docs.text:
# Check configuration
dbwarden database

# Test connection
dbwarden check-db

docs.title: Forgetting Port
docs.text:
Wrong:

database_url="postgresql://user:pass@localhost/myapp"  # Uses default port 5432

If you need a different port:

database_url="postgresql://user:pass@localhost:5433/myapp"

docs.title: Missing Slashes
docs.text:
Wrong:

database_url="sqlite://./app.db"  # Only 2 slashes

Correct:

database_url="sqlite:///./app.db"  # 3 slashes for relative path
database_url="sqlite:////absolute/path/app.db"  # 4 slashes for absolute path

docs.title: Special Characters Not Encoded
docs.text:
Wrong:

database_url="postgresql://user:p@ss@localhost/myapp"  # @ not encoded

Correct:

database_url="postgresql://user:p%40ss@localhost/myapp"  # @ encoded as %40

docs.title: Dev Mode
docs.text: Use SQLite for local development and PostgreSQL in production with the same codebase.

docs.title: What Is Dev Mode?
docs.text:
Dev mode lets you configure two database URLs: - Production URL - Used by default - Dev URL - Used when you pass --dev flag

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",    # Production
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev.db",           # Development
)

Run commands with --dev:

dbwarden --dev migrate        # Uses SQLite
dbwarden --dev status         # Uses SQLite
dbwarden migrate              # Uses PostgreSQL

docs.title: Speed
docs.text: SQLite is much faster for local development: - No network latency - No authentication overhead - File-based, not server-based - Instant startup

docs.title: Simplicity
docs.text: No PostgreSQL server required: - No Docker setup - No installation - No configuration - Works on all platforms

docs.title: Safety
docs.text: Can't accidentally affect production: - Dev database is a local file - Each developer has their own database - Easy to reset (rm dev.db) - No shared state

docs.title: Portability
docs.text: Easy to share between developers: - One config file works everywhere - No server setup instructions - Fresh developers can start immediately

docs.title: Step 1: Configure Dev Database
docs.text:
# dbwarden.py
from dbwarden import database_config

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",                    # ← Add this
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev.db",         # ← Add this
    model_paths=["app.models"],
)

docs.title: Step 2: Use Dev Mode
docs.text:
# Development workflow
dbwarden --dev make-migrations "create users"
dbwarden --dev migrate
dbwarden --dev status

# Production workflow
dbwarden make-migrations "create users"
dbwarden migrate
dbwarden status

docs.title: Daily Development
docs.text:
# Morning: pull latest code
git pull

# Run migrations against dev database
dbwarden --dev migrate

# Work on features...
# Add new models

# Generate migration
dbwarden --dev make-migrations "add orders table"

# Test migration
dbwarden --dev migrate

# Verify
dbwarden --dev status

# Commit
git add migrations/primary/0002_add_orders_table.sql
git commit -m "Add orders table"

docs.title: Testing Rollbacks
docs.text:
# Apply migration
dbwarden --dev migrate

# Test rollback
dbwarden --dev rollback

# Re-apply
dbwarden --dev migrate

docs.title: Fresh Start
docs.text:
Reset your dev database anytime:

# Delete dev database
rm dev.db

# Re-run migrations
dbwarden --dev migrate

docs.title: Production Workflow
docs.text: Dev mode only affects local development. Production uses the main URL:

docs.title: CI/CD Pipeline
docs.text:
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
- name: Run migrations
  run: dbwarden migrate  # No --dev flag
  env:
    DATABASE_URL: ${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}

docs.title: Production Deployment
docs.text:
# Staging
dbwarden migrate --database primary

# Production
dbwarden migrate --database primary

Dev mode is never used in CI/CD or production.

docs.title: What Works
docs.text: Most features work in SQLite: - ✅ Tables, columns, indexes - ✅ Primary keys, foreign keys - ✅ Unique constraints - ✅ Basic data types - ✅ Transactions

docs.title: What Doesn't Work
docs.text: Some PostgreSQL features aren't available in SQLite: - ❌ Advanced types (JSONB, arrays, enums) - ❌ Partial indexes - ❌ Generated columns (in older SQLite) - ❌ Multiple schemas - ❌ Concurrent writes

docs.title: Translation
docs.text:
DBWarden doesn't translate SQL between databases. Your migrations should work on both SQLite and PostgreSQL.

Approach 1: Write portable SQL

-- ✅ Works on both
CREATE TABLE users (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL
);

-- ❌ PostgreSQL-specific
CREATE TABLE users (
    id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    email VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    metadata JSONB
);

Approach 2: Use PostgreSQL for dev too

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
    dev_database_type="postgresql",                         # Same as prod
    dev_database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp_dev",    # Different database
)

docs.title: Automatic Dev Mode
docs.text:
Use environment variables to automatically detect dev:

import os

is_dev = os.getenv("ENV", "dev") in ["dev", "development", "local"]

if is_dev:
    database_url = "sqlite:///./dev.db"
    database_type = "sqlite"
else:
    database_url = os.getenv("DATABASE_URL")
    database_type = "postgresql"

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type=database_type,
    database_url=database_url,
)

Run commands:

# Dev
ENV=dev dbwarden migrate

# Production
ENV=production dbwarden migrate

docs.title: Multiple Dev Databases
docs.text:
If you have multiple databases, configure dev mode for each:

# Primary database
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/main",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev_primary.db",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],
)

# Analytics database
database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/analytics",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev_analytics.db",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],
)

Run against all dev databases:

dbwarden --dev migrate --all
dbwarden --dev status --all

docs.title: Pattern 1: SQLite for Dev, PostgreSQL for Prod (Recommended)
docs.text:
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev.db",
)

Pros: - Fast local iteration - No server setup - Easy to reset

Cons: - SQL must be portable - Some features unavailable in dev

docs.title: Pattern 2: PostgreSQL for Both
docs.text:
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://prod-host/myapp",
    dev_database_type="postgresql",
    dev_database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp_dev",
)

Pros: - Identical environments - Use all PostgreSQL features - Catches more bugs in dev

Cons: - Requires PostgreSQL server locally - Slower than SQLite

docs.title: Pattern 3: Dynamic Based on Environment
docs.text:
import os

environment = os.getenv("ENV", "dev")

if environment == "production":
    database_url = os.getenv("DATABASE_URL")
    database_type = "postgresql"
elif environment == "staging":
    database_url = os.getenv("STAGING_DATABASE_URL")
    database_type = "postgresql"
else:
    database_url = "sqlite:///./dev.db"
    database_type = "sqlite"

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type=database_type,
    database_url=database_url,
)

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
Use SQLite for fast unit tests:

# tests/conftest.py
import pytest
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
from app.models import Base

@pytest.fixture(scope="function")
def db():
    engine = create_engine("sqlite:///:memory:")
    Base.metadata.create_all(engine)
    yield engine
    Base.metadata.drop_all(engine)

docs.title: Integration Tests
docs.text:
Use --dev for integration tests:

# Run integration tests
dbwarden --dev migrate
pytest tests/integration/

docs.title: "SQL syntax error in SQLite"
docs.text:
Cause: Using PostgreSQL-specific SQL.

Solution: Make SQL portable or use PostgreSQL for dev:

dev_database_type="postgresql"
dev_database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp_dev"

docs.title: "dev_database_url is required"
docs.text:
Cause: Set dev_database_type without dev_database_url.

Solution: Add both:

dev_database_type="sqlite"
dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev.db"  # ← Add this

docs.title: Dev database not updating
docs.text:
Cause: Forgot --dev flag.

Solution: Use --dev:

dbwarden --dev migrate  # ← Add --dev

docs.text:
You learned:

✅ What dev mode is and why it's useful ✅ How to configure dev and production URLs ✅ Daily development workflow with --dev ✅ SQLite limitations and workarounds ✅ Environment-based configuration ✅ Common patterns and best practices ✅ Testing strategies ✅ Troubleshooting dev mode issues

docs.title: Model Discovery
docs.text: Learn how DBWarden discovers your SQLAlchemy models for migration generation.

docs.title: What Is Model Discovery?
docs.text: Model discovery is the process where DBWarden: 1. Imports Python modules 2. Finds SQLAlchemy model classes 3. Extracts table metadata 4. Uses metadata to generate migrations

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/myapp",
    model_paths=["app.models"],  # ← Discover models here
)

docs.title: What Gets Discovered
docs.text:
DBWarden looks for classes that inherit from: - DeclarativeBase (SQLAlchemy 2.0+) - declarative_base() return value (SQLAlchemy 1.4)

Example models:

# app/models.py
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column
from sqlalchemy import String, Integer

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class User(Base):  # ← Discovered
    __tablename__ = "users"
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    email: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(255))

class Order(Base):  # ← Discovered
    __tablename__ = "orders"
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(Integer, primary_key=True)

docs.title: Single Database (Optional)
docs.text:
For single-database projects, model_paths is optional:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="sqlite",
    database_url="sqlite:///./app.db",
    # No model_paths - DBWarden scans entire codebase
)

DBWarden will scan your entire codebase for models.

Single Database Recommendation

Even for single-database projects, specifying model_paths makes discovery faster and more predictable.

docs.title: Multiple Databases (Required)
docs.text:
For multi-database projects, model_paths is required for each database:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/main",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],  # ← Required
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],  # ← Required
)

Why required? To prevent ambiguity about which models belong to which database.

docs.title: Step 1: Import Modules
docs.text:
DBWarden imports each module in model_paths:

model_paths=["app.models", "app.legacy.models"]

Becomes:

import app.models
import app.legacy.models

docs.title: Step 2: Recursive Discovery
docs.text:
For each imported module, DBWarden recursively imports submodules:

app/
  models/
    __init__.py       # ← Imported
    user.py           # ← Imported
    order.py          # ← Imported
    admin/
      __init__.py     # ← Imported
      admin_user.py   # ← Imported

docs.title: Step 3: Find Model Classes
docs.text: For each module, DBWarden inspects all classes and finds those inheriting from DeclarativeBase.

docs.title: Step 4: Extract Metadata
docs.text: For each model class, DBWarden extracts: - Table name - Columns (name, type, constraints) - Indexes - Foreign keys - Check constraints - Unique constraints

docs.title: Single Module
docs.text:
model_paths=["app.models"]

Discovers models from: - app/models.py (if it's a file) - app/models/__init__.py (if it's a package) - app/models/*.py (all submodules)

docs.title: Multiple Modules
docs.text:
model_paths=["app.models", "app.legacy"]

Discovers models from both app.models and app.legacy.

docs.title: Nested Modules
docs.text:
model_paths=["app.models.api", "app.models.admin"]

Discovers models from: - app/models/api.py or app/models/api/*.py - app/models/admin.py or app/models/admin/*.py

docs.title: Absolute vs Relative
docs.text:
Absolute (recommended):

model_paths=["app.models"]  # From project root

Not supported:

model_paths=["./models"]  # Relative paths don't work
model_paths=["models"]     # May work if on PYTHONPATH

docs.title: Pattern 1: Single Module
docs.text:
app/
  models.py    # All models in one file

model_paths=["app.models"]

docs.title: Pattern 2: Module Package
docs.text:
app/
  models/
    __init__.py
    user.py
    order.py
    product.py

model_paths=["app.models"]

docs.title: Pattern 3: Multi-Database
docs.text:
app/
  models/
    primary/
      __init__.py
      user.py
      order.py
    analytics/
      __init__.py
      event.py
      metric.py

# Primary database
model_paths=["app.models.primary"]

# Analytics database  
model_paths=["app.models.analytics"]

docs.title: Pattern 4: Legacy + New
docs.text:
app/
  models/      # New models
    __init__.py
    user.py
  legacy/      # Legacy models
    models.py

model_paths=["app.models", "app.legacy.models"]

docs.title: No Overlap (Default)
docs.text:
By default, model paths cannot overlap between databases:

# ❌ Error: overlap detected
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.models"],
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models"],  # Same path!
)

docs.title: Allow Overlap
docs.text:
If models genuinely belong to multiple databases:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.shared"],
    overlap_models=True,  # ← Allow overlap
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    model_paths=["app.shared"],
    overlap_models=True,  # ← Allow overlap
)

Overlap Implications

Both databases will include the same tables. Make sure this is intentional.

docs.title: "No SQLAlchemy models found"
docs.text:
Symptom: DBWarden can't find your models.

Causes:

  1. Models not imported
# app/models/__init__.py
# ❌ Wrong - models not imported
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase

class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

# ✅ Correct - import models
from app.models.user import User
from app.models.order import Order
  1. Wrong module path
# ❌ Wrong
model_paths=["models"]  # Not on PYTHONPATH

# ✅ Correct
model_paths=["app.models"]
  1. Circular imports
# app/models/user.py
from app.models.order import Order  # ← Circular import

# app/models/order.py
from app.models.user import User  # ← Circular import

Solution: Use forward references:

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from app.models.order import Order

docs.title: "model_paths is required"
docs.text:
Symptom: Error when running commands with multiple databases.

Cause: Multiple databases configured without model_paths.

Solution: Add model_paths to each database:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],  # ← Add this
    ...
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],  # ← Add this
    ...
)

docs.title: "model_paths overlap detected"
docs.text:
Symptom: Two databases have overlapping model paths.

Cause: Same path used for multiple databases.

Solution 1: Use separate paths:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],  # Different path
    ...
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],  # Different path
    ...
)

Solution 2: Allow overlap (if intentional):

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.shared"],
    overlap_models=True,  # ← Allow overlap
    ...
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    model_paths=["app.shared"],
    overlap_models=True,  # ← Allow overlap
    ...
)

docs.title: Import Errors
docs.text:
Symptom: ModuleNotFoundError or ImportError when running commands.

Cause: DBWarden tries to import module but it doesn't exist.

Solution: Verify the module path:

python -c "import app.models"  # Test import

If import fails, fix your module structure or PYTHONPATH.

docs.title: Slow Discovery
docs.text:
If discovery is slow, reduce the search space:

Before (slow):

model_paths=["app"]  # Scans entire app

After (fast):

model_paths=["app.models"]  # Only scans models

docs.title: Import Side Effects
docs.text:
Models should be pure:

# ❌ Bad - side effects on import
class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = "users"
    ...

print("User model loaded!")  # Side effect

# ✅ Good - no side effects
class User(Base):
    __tablename__ = "users"
    ...

docs.title: Advanced: Dynamic Model Paths
docs.text:
You can compute model_paths dynamically:

import os

environment = os.getenv("ENV", "dev")

if environment == "production":
    model_paths = ["app.models.production"]
else:
    model_paths = ["app.models.dev"]

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="...",
    model_paths=model_paths,
)

docs.title: Recap
docs.text:
You learned:

docs.title: Multi-Database
docs.text: Configure and manage multiple databases in a single project.

docs.title: When to Use Multiple Databases
docs.text: Common scenarios: - Microservices - Each service has its own database - Read/Write Split - Primary for writes, replica for reads - Domain Separation - Transactions, analytics, logs in separate databases - Legacy Integration - New and old databases coexist - Multi-Tenancy - One database per tenant

docs.title: Basic Setup
docs.text:
Configure each database with database_config():

# dbwarden.py
from dbwarden import database_config

# Primary database
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/main",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],
)

# Analytics database
database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],
)

# Logging database
database_config(
    database_name="logging",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/logs",
    model_paths=["app.models.logging"],
)

docs.title: Pattern 1: Separate Modules
docs.text:
app/
  models/
    primary/
      __init__.py
      user.py
      order.py
    analytics/
      __init__.py
      event.py
      metric.py
    logging/
      __init__.py
      audit_log.py

Configuration:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],
    ...
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],
    ...
)

database_config(
    database_name="logging",
    model_paths=["app.models.logging"],
    ...
)

docs.title: Pattern 2: Shared Base Classes
docs.text:
# app/models/base.py
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase

class PrimaryBase(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

class AnalyticsBase(DeclarativeBase):
    pass

# app/models/primary/user.py
from app.models.base import PrimaryBase

class User(PrimaryBase):
    __tablename__ = "users"
    ...

# app/models/analytics/event.py
from app.models.base import AnalyticsBase

class Event(AnalyticsBase):
    __tablename__ = "events"
    ...

docs.title: Target Specific Database
docs.text:
# Migrate primary
dbwarden migrate --database primary

# Migrate analytics
dbwarden migrate --database analytics

# Status for logging
dbwarden status --database logging

docs.title: Target All Databases
docs.text:
# Migrate all
dbwarden migrate --all

# Status for all
dbwarden status --all

# Rollback all
dbwarden rollback --all

docs.title: Default Database
docs.text:
The database with default=True is used when --database is omitted:

# These are equivalent when primary is default:
dbwarden migrate
dbwarden migrate --database primary

docs.title: Migration Directories
docs.text:
Each database has its own migration directory:

migrations/
  primary/
    0001_create_users.sql
    0002_create_orders.sql
  analytics/
    0001_create_events.sql
    0002_create_metrics.sql
  logging/
    0001_create_audit_logs.sql

Configure custom directories:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    migrations_dir="migrations/primary",  # Custom path
    ...
)

docs.title: Independent Migration Histories
docs.text:
Each database maintains its own migration history:

# Check primary history
$ dbwarden history --database primary
Applied Migrations (primary)
  0001_create_users (2024-01-15 10:30:00)
  0002_create_orders (2024-01-16 11:00:00)

# Check analytics history
$ dbwarden history --database analytics
Applied Migrations (analytics)
  0001_create_events (2024-01-15 10:35:00)

Migrations are completely independent - you can migrate one database without affecting others.

docs.title: Dev Mode with Multiple Databases
docs.text:
Configure dev mode for each database:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/main",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev_primary.db",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],
)

database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/analytics",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev_analytics.db",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],
)

Use dev mode:

# Dev mode for all databases
dbwarden --dev migrate --all

# Dev mode for specific database
dbwarden --dev migrate --database analytics

docs.title: Pattern 1: Read/Write Split
docs.text:
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://primary-host/myapp",
    model_paths=["app.models"],
)

database_config(
    database_name="replica",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://replica-host/myapp",
    model_paths=["app.models"],  # Same models
    overlap_models=True,          # Allow overlap
)

Note: Run migrations only against primary; replica replicates automatically.

docs.title: Pattern 2: Domain Separation
docs.text:
# Transactions
database_config(
    database_name="transactions",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/transactions",
    model_paths=["app.models.transactions"],
)

# Analytics
database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    database_type="clickhouse",
    database_url="http://localhost:8123/analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],
)

# Audit logs
database_config(
    database_name="audit",
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://localhost/audit",
    model_paths=["app.models.audit"],
)

docs.title: Pattern 3: Multi-Tenant
docs.text:
tenants = ["tenant_a", "tenant_b", "tenant_c"]

for tenant in tenants:
    database_config(
        database_name=tenant,
        default=(tenant == "tenant_a"),
        database_type="postgresql",
        database_url=f"postgresql://localhost/{tenant}",
        model_paths=["app.models"],  # Same models for all tenants
    )

docs.title: Required: model_paths
docs.text:
When you have multiple databases, each must specify model_paths:

# ❌ Error: model_paths required
database_config(database_name="primary", ...)
database_config(database_name="analytics", ...)  # Missing model_paths

# ✅ Correct
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],
    ...
)
database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models.analytics"],
    ...
)

docs.title: No Overlap (Default)
docs.text:
Model paths cannot overlap:

# ❌ Error: overlap detected
database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.models"],
    ...
)
database_config(
    database_name="analytics",
    model_paths=["app.models"],  # Same path
    ...
)

docs.title: Allow Overlap
docs.text:
For read replicas or shared models:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.models"],
    overlap_models=True,  # ← Allow overlap
    ...
)
database_config(
    database_name="replica",
    model_paths=["app.models"],
    overlap_models=True,  # ← Allow overlap
    ...
)

docs.title: "model_paths is required"
docs.text:
Solution: Add model_paths to all databases:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    model_paths=["app.models.primary"],  # ← Add this
    ...
)

docs.title: "model_paths overlap detected"
docs.text:
Solution 1: Use separate paths:

model_paths=["app.models.primary"]
model_paths=["app.models.analytics"]

Solution 2: Allow overlap:

overlap_models=True

docs.title: Wrong database targeted
docs.text:
Check default:

dbwarden database  # Shows which is default

Be explicit:

dbwarden migrate --database analytics  # Specify database

docs.title: Recap
docs.text: ✅ Configure multiple databases with separate database_config() calls ✅ Organize models in separate modules ✅ Each database has independent migration history ✅ Target specific database with --database flag ✅ Target all databases with --all flag ✅ Configure dev mode per database ✅ model_paths required for multi-database

docs.title: Deployment with Init Container
docs.text:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      initContainers:
      # Run migrations before app starts
      - name: migrate
        image: myapp:latest
        command: ["dbwarden", "migrate"]
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: database-secret
              key: url

      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myapp:latest
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: database-secret
              key: url

docs.title: RDS with Secrets Manager
docs.text:
import os
import json
import boto3

def get_database_url():
    secret_name = os.getenv("DB_SECRET_NAME")
    region = os.getenv("AWS_REGION", "us-east-1")

    client = boto3.client("secretsmanager", region_name=region)
    response = client.get_secret_value(SecretId=secret_name)
    secret = json.loads(response["SecretString"])

    return (
        f"postgresql://{secret['username']}:{secret['password']}"
        f"@{secret['host']}:{secret['port']}/{secret['dbname']}"
    )

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=get_database_url(),
    model_paths=["app.models"],
    secure_values=True,
)

docs.title: RDS Connection via IAM
docs.text:
import os
import boto3

def get_rds_auth_token():
    rds_client = boto3.client("rds")
    return rds_client.generate_db_auth_token(
        DBHostname=os.getenv("DB_HOST"),
        Port=5432,
        DBUsername=os.getenv("DB_USER"),
    )

database_url = (
    f"postgresql://{os.getenv('DB_USER')}:{get_rds_auth_token()}"
    f"@{os.getenv('DB_HOST')}:5432/{os.getenv('DB_NAME')}"
)

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url=database_url,
    model_paths=["app.models"],
)

docs.title: Environment-Based Configuration
docs.text:
import os

ENVIRONMENT = os.getenv("ENVIRONMENT", "dev")

if ENVIRONMENT == "production":
    database_url = os.getenv("PROD_DATABASE_URL")
    database_type = "postgresql"
elif ENVIRONMENT == "staging":
    database_url = os.getenv("STAGING_DATABASE_URL")
    database_type = "postgresql"
else:
    database_url = "sqlite:///./dev.db"
    database_type = "sqlite"

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type=database_type,
    database_url=database_url,
    model_paths=["app.models"],
    secure_values=(ENVIRONMENT != "dev"),
)

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Configure your first database in 2 minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: You should have: - Python 3.10+ installed - DBWarden installed (pip install dbwarden) - A database to connect to (or use SQLite)

docs.title: Step 1: Initialize
docs.text:
Create project structure:

dbwarden init

This creates: - migrations/ directory - dbwarden.py configuration file

docs.title: Step 2: Your First Configuration
docs.text:
Open dbwarden.py and add:

from dbwarden import database_config

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="sqlite",
    database_url="sqlite:///./app.db",
)

That's it! 4 required parameters: - database_name - What to call this database - default - Is this the default? - database_type - What kind of database? - database_url - How to connect?

Use SQLite First

Start with SQLite for the simplest setup. Switch to PostgreSQL later.

docs.title: Step 3: Test the Configuration
docs.text:
Verify DBWarden can read your config:

dbwarden database

You'll see:

Database Configuration
════════════════════════════════════════

primary (default)
  Type: sqlite
  URL: sqlite:///./app.db
  Migrations: migrations/primary

docs.title: Step 4: Add Model Paths (Optional)
docs.text:
If you have SQLAlchemy models, tell DBWarden where they are:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="sqlite",
    database_url="sqlite:///./app.db",
    model_paths=["app.models"],  # ← Add this
)

DBWarden will discover models from app.models and its submodules.

docs.title: Step 5: Upgrade to PostgreSQL
docs.text:
When you're ready for PostgreSQL:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/myapp",
    model_paths=["app.models"],
)

docs.title: Step 6: Add Dev Mode (Recommended)
docs.text:
Keep SQLite for local dev, use PostgreSQL in production:

database_config(
    database_name="primary",
    default=True,
    database_type="postgresql",
    database_url="postgresql://user:password@localhost:5432/myapp",
    dev_database_type="sqlite",
    dev_database_url="sqlite:///./dev.db",
    model_paths=["app.models"],
)

Now you can run commands against SQLite locally:

dbwarden --dev migrate
dbwarden --dev status

And against PostgreSQL in production:

dbwarden migrate
dbwarden status

docs.title: database_config() Registered Your Database
docs.text: When Python loads dbwarden.py, it executes database_config() which: 1. Validates your parameters 2. Registers the database in DBWarden's internal registry 3. Sets up migration directories

docs.title: DBWarden Can Now Find Your Database
docs.text:
All CLI commands now know about your database:

dbwarden make-migrations "create users"
dbwarden migrate
dbwarden status
dbwarden history

docs.title: "No configuration found"
docs.text:
Cause: DBWarden can't find dbwarden.py

Solution: Ensure you're in the project directory and dbwarden.py exists.

docs.title: "No SQLAlchemy models found"
docs.text:
Cause: DBWarden can't discover your models

Solution: Add model_paths to your config:

model_paths=["app.models"]

docs.title: "Exactly one default=True required"
docs.text:
Cause: Multiple databases without one marked as default

Solution: Set one database to default=True

docs.text:
